join_order.notes: is_big[] dense bool array size 60000001 (~57MB as bool, or 7.5MB as bitset); O(1) membership test per orders row; array fits in L3 cache (44MB) after warm-up
Dense array index 5.7MB; only ~2,520 lookups total — negligible cost; resolves c_name and c_custkey in one cache-hot load

filters.predicate: SUM(l_quantity) GROUP BY l_orderkey HAVING SUM > 300
filters.notes: Pass 1 HAVING filter applied after full lineitem scan; ~2,520 qualifying orderkeys from ~60M distinct values
Evaluated via is_big[o_orderkey] direct array lookup; ~2,520 rows survive from 15M

data_structures.pass1_qty_accumulator: dense_array(int32_t, size=60000001, ~229MB); thread-local partial arrays merged by element-wise sum after parallel scan
data_structures.qualifying_filter: dense_array(bool, size=60000001, ~57MB as char or use bitset 7.5MB); derived from qty_accumulator after merge; fits in L3 cache (44MB) as bitset

pass1_reuse_optimization.description: The subquery SUM(l_quantity) per l_orderkey and the outer query SUM(l_quantity) per group are identical — both aggregate ALL lineitem rows for qualifying orderkeys. The qty_accumulator built in Pass 1 already contains the correct final sum_qty values. No second lineitem scan is needed for aggregation.

parallelism.pass1_lineitem_scan: parallel morsel scan of l_orderkey + l_quantity; each thread owns a thread-local int32_t partial[60000001]; merge via element-wise sum after all threads finish
parallelism.pass2_orders_scan: parallel morsel scan of orders columns (o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice); each thread collects local Q18Result vector for qualifying rows; merge vectors after scan
parallelism.sort: single-threaded partial_sort or nth_element+sort on ~2520 elements (trivially fast)

indexes.notes: Resolves o_custkey -> c_name row in O(1); 5.7MB fully L3-resident; invoked ~2,520 times

fields.name: o_orderdate
fields.source: orders/o_orderdate.bin (days_since_epoch; decode to YYYY-MM-DD for output only)
